coredata.dc:title: Antioxidant activity and immunocompatibility of Maillard reaction carbon dots derived from arginine-glucose model system

coredata.dc:description: The immunocompatibility and antioxidative characteristics of Maillard reaction-based carbon dots (MR-CDs) have not been extensively studied. This study aimed to develop a Maillard reaction model system utilizing glucose and arginine to synthesize carbon dots. The investigation into the MR-CD’s physicochemical properties, biocompatibility, cytoprotective effects, and antioxidant capacity revealed promising results. TEM images showed spherical carbon dots (CDs) of 3–15 nm diameter. The FTIR spectra indicated the presence of hydroxyl, amino, and carbonyl functional groups in MR-CDs surfaces. An X-ray diffractometer analysis showed that CDs are amorphous. The fluorescence spectra revealed wavelength-dependent emission with a maximum intensity of 390 nm. The response of THP-1 cells after exposure to MR-CDs was studied to assess biocompatibility, nuclear morphology, ROS levels, lysosome accumulation, and gene expression of inflammatory markers (IL6, TNFα, NF-κB, MIF, IFNA, and GSR genes). MR-CDs induced a dose-dependent proliferation of THP-1 cells without altering their nuclear morphology. A lysosome and mitochondrial membrane potential analysis also showed that MR-CDs are non-toxic. The levels of intracellular ROS did not differ from those in the control. Downregulation of inflammatory genes was observed after exposure to MR-CDs. The expression of the Glutathione-disulfide reductase (GSR) gene was upregulated in a dose-dependent manner, indicating increased antioxidant activity in MR-CDs. The result of the DPPH assay confirmed the antioxidant ability of MR-CDs. MR-CDs performed cytoprotective effects against hydrogen peroxide-induced cell damage. These findings suggest that, besides their immunocompatibility, MR-CDs possess potent antioxidant and cytoprotective properties, which make them ideal for a range of immunomodulatory applications, sparking optimism for future research and development in this field.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The immunocompatibility and antioxidative characteristics of Maillard reaction-based carbon dots (MR-CDs) have not been extensively studied. This study aimed to develop a Maillard reaction model system utilizing glucose and arginine to synthesize carbon dots. The investigation into the MR-CD’s physicochemical properties, biocompatibility, cytoprotective effects, and antioxidant capacity revealed promising results. TEM images showed spherical carbon dots (CDs) of 3–15 nm diameter. The FTIR spectra indicated the presence of hydroxyl, amino, and carbonyl functional groups in MR-CDs surfaces. An X-ray diffractometer analysis showed that CDs are amorphous. The fluorescence spectra revealed wavelength-dependent emission with a maximum intensity of 390 nm. The response of THP-1 cells after exposure to MR-CDs was studied to assess biocompatibility, nuclear morphology, ROS levels, lysosome accumulation, and gene expression of inflammatory markers (IL6, TNFα, NF-κB, MIF, IFNA, and GSR genes). MR-CDs induced a dose-dependent proliferation of THP-1 cells without altering their nuclear morphology. A lysosome and mitochondrial membrane potential analysis also showed that MR-CDs are non-toxic. The levels of intracellular ROS did not differ from those in the control. Downregulation of inflammatory genes was observed after exposure to MR-CDs. The expression of the Glutathione-disulfide reductase (GSR) gene was upregulated in a dose-dependent manner, indicating increased antioxidant activity in MR-CDs. The result of the DPPH assay confirmed the antioxidant ability of MR-CDs. MR-CDs performed cytoprotective effects against hydrogen peroxide-induced cell damage. These findings suggest that, besides their immunocompatibility, MR-CDs possess potent antioxidant and cytoprotective properties, which make them ideal for a range of immunomodulatory applications, sparking optimism for future research and development in this field.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a type of small carbon nanoparticles, measuring less than 10 nm, that have a wide range of applications, including bioimaging, biosensing, drug delivery, cancer therapy, photoactivity, supercapacitors, catalysts, and fuel cells (Harish et al., 2022; Sameer et al., 2023). Due to their tunable photoluminescence, excellent photostability, and facile surface modification, CDs are valuable diagnostic and therapeutic tool (Goryacheva et al., 2017; Xiao & Sun, 2018). These nanomaterials can be modified to achieve various functions, including antioxidant and fluorescent properties, biocompatibility, enhanced stability, improved solubility, drug loading, targeting capabilities and multimodal imaging functionalities (Koutsogiannis et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2020). For instance, Goji berries were preserved through photodynamic treatment with CDs derived from citric acid and ethylenediamine (Du et al., 2024). Ni et al. also suggested that CDs have been used as photosensitizers to extend the shelf life of grapes (Ni et al., 2024).

Identifying nanomaterials with antioxidative or oxidative properties is essential in nanomedicine (Innocenzi & Stagi, 2023; Murru et al., 2020). Oxidative stress has been associated with neurodegenerative diseases, cardiovascular diseases, cancer, and ageing (Moylan & Reid, 2007). An antioxidative nanomaterial could potentially play a therapeutic role by mitigating oxidative damage. These antioxidant nanomaterials can scavenge reactive oxygen species, inhibit lipid peroxidation, and protect cells from oxidative stress, emphasizing their importance in designing innovative biomedical treatments for oxidative stress (Akhtar et al., 2017; Valgimigli et al., 2018). Moreover, integrating antioxidative nanomaterials into drug delivery systems and biomedical devices may provide a promising approach to multiple health problems.

The Maillard reaction (MR) is a complex, non-enzymatic chemical browning reaction between amino acids and reducing sugars, producing a wide range of aromatic and flavorful compounds. This reaction was first described in 1878 by French scientist Louis Camille Maillard (Tamanna & Mahmood, 2015). In addition to their culinary applications and unique properties, the Maillard reaction is strongly linked to the formation of CDs that can exhibit unique properties (Li, Liu, et al., 2018; Wei et al., 2014). Moreover, generating CDs through MR offers simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and the potential to tailor their properties. Under controlled conditions, specific precursors, such as reducing sugars and amino acids, are converted into CDs with distinct physicochemical characteristics, such as tunable optical and antioxidative properties (Sk et al., 2012). Thus, we have selected glucose and arginine as biocompatible precursors for the synthesis of Maillard reaction-derived carbon dots (MR-CDs) due to their abundance, low toxicity, and inherent antioxidative properties. Glucose is a natural reducing sugar and a key ingredient in the Maillard reaction, providing the carbon source necessary for forming CDs. Arginine is involved in the Maillard reaction, as well as surface passivation and functionalization for CDs. Combining glucose and arginine as precursors makes it possible to produce CDs of enhanced biocompatibility and potentially suitable candidates for use in biological systems.

Evidence suggests that the surface characteristics of CDs play a crucial role in their interaction with different cell types. For instance, immune cells can recognize and bind to the surface of CDs based on the functional groups present on their surface. Various endocytic pathways can internalize CDs by immune cells, including macrophages, dendritic cells, and lymphocytes (Orecchioni et al., 2014; Yuan et al., 2019). The cellular uptake mechanism for CDs is determined by their surface chemistry, charge, and size (M. Zhang et al., 2018). Following internalization, CDs are transported through endosomes and lysosomes for intracellular trafficking (Stern, Adiseshaiah, & Crist, 2012).

Understanding the fate of CDs on immune cells is crucial to predict their biological effects. Studies have shown that CDs influence immune cell function and have immunomodulatory effects, including modulation of cytokine production, activation of immune cells, and antigen-presenting cells (Najafi-Hajivar et al., 2016; Pandey & Prajapati, 2018). Kumar et al. (2024) demonstrated that citric acid-derived carbon dots reduce inflammation and reactive oxygen species. Li et al. found that glucose and tetraethylenepentamine-derived CDs functionalized with ricin toxin binding subunit B promoted immunomodulation in RAW264.7 cells (Li, Liu, et al., 2018). In addition, herbal tea CDs showed immunoregulatory activities attributed to their significant role in activating inflammation-related proteins iNOS and COX-2 (Fu et al., 2022).

Besides their biomedical application potential, MR-CDs are food-derived CDs and therefore, evaluating their immunocompatibility and the subsequent suitability for biomedical applications is of great importance. Thus, the biocompatibility and the bioactivity of MR-CDs must be assessed using an in-vitro human cells-based model. The THP-1 cell line, one of the most widely used human monocytic cell lines, is an excellent model for studying cellular responses to nanomaterials. Biocompatibility studies on THP-1 cells provide valuable information on CDs immunomodulatory properties, thereby aiding their safety profile and usefulness for biomedical applications. Hence, in this study, we extracted CDs from glucose/arginine-based Maillard reaction to examine their antioxidant features, cellular interactions, cytotoxicity, internalization mechanisms, and immunomodulatory effects on THP-1 monocytic cells.

Alpha-D glucose was supplied by Across Organic Co. Arginine was obtained from Alpha Chemika Co. THP-1 cells were obtained from the American Type Culture Collection (ATCC, Manassas, VA, USA). MTT dye [3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2, 5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide], acridine orange (AO), ethidium bromide (EB), fetal bovine serum (FBS), and DMEM were obtained from Sigma, USA. Trypsin and other cell culture materials were supplied by Gibco, Paisley, UK. cDNA kit and primers were purchased from QIAGEN (Hilden, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon dots fabrication from Maillard reaction
ce:section.ce:para: Arginine-glucose was mixed (volume ratio of 2:1) with double distilled water for the Maillard reaction model. About 2 g of arginine and 1 g of D-glucose were dissolved in 50 mL of distilled water and mixed well under stirring. Then, the mixture was kept in the oven at 250 °C for different durations (15, 25, and 30 min). The obtained brownish product was cooled at room temperature (24 °C) and then redissolved in distilled water. Next, samples were centrifuged at 8000×g at room temperature (24 °C) for 10 min to eliminate possible large insoluble particles. The supernatants were then filtered using a syringe filter (0.22 μm). Next, the obtained filtrates were purified by liquid-liquid extraction using water and chloroform. The water fraction was dialyzed for further purification and then freeze-dried to obtain MR-CDs powder. CDs were labelled according to reaction time (AG15, AG25, and AG30).

The size and shape of MR-CDs particles was investigated using transmission electron microscopy (JEOL-2100, Japan). The absorption spectrum of the MR-CDs solution was analyzed by a UV–Vis–NIR spectrophotometer (Agilent, USA). The fluorescence behavior of the MR-CDs solution was investigated under different excitations from 310 nm to 520 nm using fluorescence spectroscopy (Jasco FP-8300 spectrofluorometer). The Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Tensor 27, Jasco Inc., Easton, MD, USA) was used within the wavelength range of 4000–400 cm−1 to detect the functional groups of the MR-CDs. X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku MiniFlex) operated with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) was used to analyze the crystalline structures of the MR-CDs (Athinarayanan et al., 2020).

Human leukaemia monocytic cells (THP-1) were grown in DMEM with 10% FBS, antibiotics in 25 cm2 and T75 cm2 flasks, and 96-well culture plates at 37 °C in 5% CO2. All experiments were carried out using cells from passage 15 or less. The ultrafine carbon nanostructures were sonicated with a cell culture medium for in-vitro studies.

In a 96-well plate, THP-1 cells were seeded in 200 μL of medium at a density of 15,000 cells per well (Periasamy et al., 2024). Afterwards, THP-1 cells were treated with different MR-CDs concentrations (0, 12.5, 25, 50, 100, and 200 μg/mL) and incubated for 24 and 48 h. Next, 20 μl of MTT solution (5 mg/mL in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS)) was added to each well. After incubation of 6 h at 37 °C, the supernatant was replaced with 100 μL of DMSO in each well. A 96-well plate reader (Bio-Rad, CA, USA) was used to measure the absorbance at 570 nm and 630 nm (measurement and reference, respectively). The experiment was performed in triplicates to calculate the mean. The cell survival rate was calculated according to the following formula:

The cells were exposed to different doses of MR-CDs for 24 and 48h. At the end of the incubation period, the plate was centrifuged at 2400×g at room temperature (24 °C) for 5 min, and the media was carefully removed. Then, cells were stained with dual-stain acridine orange/ethidium bromide (AO/EB). Stained cells were visualized and photographed under fluorescence microscopy (Alomari et al., 2023).

The fluorescent probe JC-1 was used to assess the mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP) of MR-CDs treated THP-1 cells. JC-1 monomers emit green fluorescence with low MMP, whereas J-aggregates emit red-orange fluorescence, indicating high MMP (Perelman et al., 2012). THP-1 cells were plated at a density of 5 × 104 cells per well in 12-well plates. Then, MR-CDs were added to the cells (Control, 100, and 200 μg/ml) and incubated for 24h. The plate was then centrifuged, and the media was removed. Next, JC-1 was added to each well and incubated for 30 min. The MMP activity of THP-1 cells was analyzed using fluorescent microscopy (Carl Zeiss, USA).

THP-1 cells were exposed to different doses of MR-CDs and incubated for 24 h. Next, the cells were labelled with LysoRed for 15 min. The lysosomal activity of the treated cell was examined under a fluorescence microscope (Alkharashi et al., 2017).

The THP-1 cells were exposed to different concentrations of MR-CDs and incubated for 24 h. The plate was then centrifuged, and the media was removed. After applying the Hoechst 33,342 stain, the plate was kept in the dark for 15 min. A fluorescence microscope was used to identify nuclear changes in THP-1 cells.

Based on the manufacturer’s instructions (ThermoFisher Scientific, USA), DCFDA (2′,7′- dichloro fluorescein diacetate) fluorescent probes were utilized to evaluate the intracellular ROS generation in MR-CDs treated THP-1 cells. In the presence of reactive oxygen species, 2′,7′- dichlorofluorescein diacetate is oxidized to dichlorofluorescein (DCF), which emits green fluorescence (Tetz et al., 2014). After 24h of MR-CDs treatment, the plate was centrifuged, and the culture media was removed. DCFDA dye was added to the cells and incubated in the dark at 37 °C for 30 min. Fluorescence microscopy was then used to examine ROS formation inside the cells.

THP-1 cells were grown and treated with MR-CDs for 24 h. After treatment, cDNA was synthesized with the Fastlane® Cell cDNA kit (QIAGEN, Germany). The mRNA levels of the targeted genes (GSR, IL6, TNFα, NF-κB, MIF, and IFNA) were assessed using GAPDH as a housekeeping gene. In each well of a 96-well PCR plate, 12.5 μl of the master mix was mixed with 2 μl of cDNA (500 ng), 2 μl of primer, and 4 μl of RNase-free water. The real-time qPCR reactions were run for 40 cycles. The acquired data was analyzed using the comparative threshold (Ct) method. The expression of the target gene was measured in treated cells and compared to the control cells. We calculated the target gene expression level and normalized it to the expression of GAPDH, the reference gene. To calculate the reference gene to the target genes expression ratio, as well as the relative expression levels, the following formulas were applied:
ΔCt = Ct (target genes) – Ct (GAPDH) and ΔΔCt = ΔCt (Treated) – ΔCt (Control).
The resulting data were used to plot the expression of the targeted genes using the expression 2−ΔΔCt.

The scavenging activity of MR-CDs was measured using a DPPH assay. In a 96-well microplate, 150 μl of DPPH solution (0.2 mM in absolute ethanol) was added to 50 μl of various concentrations of MR-CDs solution (50, 100, 200, and 250 μg/ml). After incubation at room temperature for 30 min, the absorbance was measured at 517 nm. The scavenging rate was calculated using the following equation:
DPPH scavenging rate (%) = [1- (Abs sample – Abs blank) / Abs control] × 100).
The sample is the DPPH solution added to the MR-CDs or the ascorbic acid solution (as a positive control), and the blank is the ethanol added to the MR-CDs solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytoprotective effect of CDs against hydrogen peroxide-induced damage
ce:section.ce:para: First, the response of cells to different concentrations of H2O2 exposure was used to establish the oxidative damage model. After determining the effective dose, another 96-well plate was used for cell seeding. After 24h incubation, the cells were treated with 1 mM of H2O2 as well as different concentrations of CDs (12.5, 25, 100, 200 μg/ml) and incubated for 3h before the cell viability assay. Then, 20 μl of MTT (5 mg mL−1) was added and incubated for 4h. DMSO (150 μl) was added after removing the supernatant, and the absorbance at 570 nm was measured.

The obtained data are presented as mean ± standard deviation (SD). Paired t-test is used for statistical significance. A p-value of ≤ 0.05 was considered statistically significant. The data was calculated using Microsoft Office Excel.

The present study adopts the Maillard reaction to produce CDs using glucose and arginine as precursors. The physicochemical properties of the Maillard reaction-derived CDs (MR-CDs) were investigated using different techniques. We also performed a biocompatibility and assessment on the monocyte model using various in-vitro assays. Scheme 1 represents the synthesis method used and the resulting gradient brown colors obtained by increasing the reaction time (15, 25 and 30 min) as well as the fluorescence of the produced MR-CDs under UV light.

It is worth mentioning that the synthesis method we used is a simulation of the cooking method where we apply reasonable heating duration and an average baking temperature. The rationale for mimicking cooking is to investigate the producibility of MR-CDs in daily cooked food with a minimum cooking time of 15 min. Indeed, our results indicate a successful formation of carbon dots in all fractions, which can be clearly seen in TEM images (Fig. 1 A), where we observed spherical-shaped CDs with diameters ranging from 3 to 15 nm. No significant differences are seen between the TEM images of AG15, AG25, and AG30 samples.

Crystalline characteristics investigated by X-ray diffractometer (XRD) analysis revealed that the MR-CDs have an amorphous nature with a broad peak at 21° (Fig. 1B). The peaks represent the graphitic structure of MR-CDs. This study suggests that MR-CDs are amorphous and similar in structure to other carbon-based nanomaterial, consistent with previous findings (Cao et al., 2017; Eskalen et al., 2021).

The optical features of the prepared CDs were investigated using UV–Vis spectroscopy (Fig. 2 ). AG15 samples show a peak of around 260 nm, while AG25 and AG30 samples show a band of about 303 nm. At 260 nm, the absorption band is caused by a π-π∗ transition of a C=C bond, whereas at 303 nm, the absorption band represents an n-π∗ transition of the C=O bond.

The fluorescence spectrum patterns of the MR-CDs indicate homogeneous fluorescence characteristics (Fig. 4 ). All three MR-CDs exhibit wavelength-dependent emission with bathochromic shift and a maximum emission of 470 nm at the excitation wavelength of 390 nm. The excitation-dependent multicolor emission of CDs was explained based on the selective excitation of different sizes corresponding to different dominant bandgaps. (Goryacheva et al., 2017; Kang & Lee, 2019). Recent investigations, however, suggest that the excitation-dependent PL phenomenon of CDs is attributed to electron transitions that are specifically mediated by oxygen-related surface states (El-Shafey, 2021; Siddique et al., 2021). This distinctive behavior allows easy regulation of PL by only controlling the excitation wavelength (Liu et al., 2020; López-Beltrán et al., 2023). Thus, MR-CDs can ideally fit in multicolor bioimaging and other fluorescent-based applications, including biosensing, drug delivery, and photodynamic therapy. (Mohammadi et al., 2022; Sargazi et al., 2022).

The cytocompatibility of THP-1 cells was examined with MR-CDs. Interestingly, THP-1 cells treated with MR-CDs exhibited remarkable proliferation in dose-response patterns (Fig. 5 A&B). A potential relationship between surface functional groups of MR-CDs and cell proliferation offers an intriguing avenue for understanding the mechanisms underlying observed proliferation. THP-1 cell proliferation may be modulated by specific functional groups (peptide mimicking) created during the MR and attached to MR-CDs. In accordance with Avolio et al. (2022), who suggested that short peptides influence the proliferation of THP-1 cells (Avolio et al., 2022). Another explanation is that cell proliferation is attributed to the antioxidant properties of MR-CDs. Several studies in different cell lines have suggested a relationship between antioxidant treatment and cellular proliferation. Antioxidants can influence cell survival rate by mitigating oxidative stress through neutralizing free radicals, increasing the expression of antioxidant enzymes as well as increasing genomic stability (Shaban et al., 2017; Taroncher et al., 2023).

Subsequently, we examined the impact of MR-CDs on nuclear morphology, mitochondrial membrane potential, and intracellular ROS levels in THP-1 cells. An AO/EB staining showed that AG MR-CDs exposure was not toxic, as indicated by intact nuclei appearing in green (Fig. 5C). Additionally, Hoechst staining confirms the AO/EB findings, showing that treated cells do not exhibit any nuclear morphological changes (Fig. 6 ). In addition, the accumulation of MR-CDs in intracellular lysosomes is similar to that of the control group (Fig. 6). Intact nuclei and lysosomes indicate the absence of cellular stress or damage, further supporting the compounds' favorable cytocompatibility. These findings suggest the tested compounds are biocompatible, emphasizing their potential use in various biomedical and cellular applications.

Mitochondrial Membrane Potential (MMP) has provided valuable insights into the function and health of mitochondria. Based on the JC-1 staining, none of the samples were depleted of MMP (Fig. 6). Mitochondrial function and health are crucial to various cellular processes, including energy production and signaling. Furthermore, no changes were observed in the levels of reactive oxygen species (ROS) within the cells, indicating biocompatibility and low levels of oxidative stress (Fig. 9
). According to these findings, the MR-CDs tested are biocompatible and have minimal effects on intracellular ROS levels. Our findings have provided a deeper understanding of MR-CD’s impact on THP-1 cells.

Gene expression analysis was carried out to investigate the effect of MR-CDs on inflammatory and antioxidant response genes. The results showed (Fig. 7) that MR-CDs promote downregulation of TNFα (with 100 μg/ml) and significant suppression of IL6, dose-dependent decrease in IFNα and no significant effect on NF-κB. On the other hand, the transcription level of the GSR gene was significantly up-regulated in a dose-dependent manner, with a 4-fold increase in mRNA level at the higher dose of MR-CDs (100 μg/ml).

Downregulation of the genes IL6, TNFα and IFNa suggests the potential suppression of cytokines and inflammatory signaling pathways. Consequently, these findings indicate that the MR-CDs may possess anti-inflammatory properties. On the other hand, the dose-dependent upregulation of GSR indicates enhanced expression of glutathione reductase, which is essential for cellular antioxidant defense. The antioxidant properties of these MR-CDs may contribute to the significant increase in the antioxidant capacity of the cells. Overall, MR-CDs modulate genes coding for inflammatory and antioxidant responses, suggesting that they may exert anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. This study provides valuable insight into the molecular mechanisms underlying cellular responses to MR-CDs and their potential implications for various biomedical and therapeutic applications.

The CDs are known to exhibit intrinsic antioxidative properties that scavenge reactive oxygen species, inhibit oxidative damage, and protect cells from oxidative stress (Janus, L. et al., 2020). Indeed, CDs show a significant variability in their radical scavenging activity according to composition and structure. They also exhibit different performances depending on the type of radicals (Innocenzi & Stagi, 2023). Although the antioxidant properties of MR products are well documented in the literature (Benjakul et al., 2005; Gu et al., 2009), only a few studies have assessed the antioxidant ability of CDs generated from MR (Li et al., 2018, 2020). Therefore, it is imperative to investigate whether Arginine-Glucose MR-CDs can mitigate oxidative stress-related pathologies to advance therapeutic interventions and disease management. The antiradical activity towards reactive nitrogen species (RNS), NO. and NO2, which can interfere with the cellular activity, is evaluated by measuring the radical content in solutions containing the CDs compared to control without. The standard measure for this purpose is 1,1-Diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl radical (DPPH•), a stable, nitrogen-centered free radical molecule (Ionita, 2021).

The result of this assessment shows (Fig. 8) that Arg25 MR-CDs could significantly scavenge DPPH radical by 23% at the lower concentration (50 μg/ml, P < 0.01), while nearly reaching a plateau of 20% across higher concentrations. On the other hand, AG30 achieved a significantly higher scavenging rate that positively correlated with elevated concentrations, where 250 μg/ml reduced DPPH by 41% (P < 0.01).

Nitrogen in the DPPH molecule has two lone-pair electrons surrounded by three benzene rings. Because of its electronic configuration, DPPH can easily accept a H• to form the stable diamagnetic DPPH-H complex (Ionita, 2021). An explanation for the possible mechanism of the radical scavenging abilities of CDs is linked to hydrogen transfer from MR-CDs surface groups to DPPH radicals. Specifically, the presence of carboxyls (-COOH), hydroxyls (-OH) and amino (-NH2, -NH) groups facilitate the hydrogen transfer and, therefore, reduction of DPPH to DPPH-H. The unpaired electrons on the surface of CDs can be delocalized by resonance within the aromatic domains or through the rearrangement of chemical bonds (Innocenzi & Stagi, 2023). Therefore, we can argue that the higher antioxidant activity of AG30 may account for the richest content of the carboxyl group observed by FTIR analysis. Furthermore, early studies proposed that the antioxidant activity of MR is positively associated with browning intensity as the intermediates or the final brown compounds of MR can serve as hydrogen donors (Benjakul et al., 2005; Kirigaya et al., 1968).

The IC50 (half-maximal inhibitory concentration) is a measure of the concentration of a compound required to inhibit a particular biological or biochemical process by 50%. It also used to evaluate and compare the antioxidant capacity of compounds (Dontha, 2016). The AG30 MR-CDs, which have the highest browning intensity and higher antioxidant activity, exhibited an IC50 of 376 μg/ml. Compared with an IC50 of 570 μg/ml reported for the MR-CDs derived from glucose and Lys (Li et al., 2018). Lower IC50 values indicate a higher antioxidant potential. This investigation revealed that the MR-CDs exhibit high DPPH scavenging capacity, dosage-dependent antioxidant activity, and a positive correlation with browning intensity, positioning AG30 MR-CDs as promising candidates for applications demanding strong antioxidant properties (Anwar et al., 2019; W. Wang et al., 2014; Xu et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The cytoprotective capability of MR-CDs against oxidative damage induced by H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: Oxidative stress occurs as a result of excessive generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), causing cell damage as well as a disturbance in normal cellular functions, which contributes to cardiovascular, liver, neural, cancer, kidney, and gastrointestinal diseases (Moylan & Reid, 2007; Sharifi-Rad et al., 2020). Antioxidant-based therapy has numerous benefits, including neuroprotection, disease amelioration, metabolic disorders, and mitochondrial dysfunction (Sharifi-Rad et al., 2020). In recent years, nanoparticle-based antioxidant therapies have emerged as promising research areas. The efficient cellular uptake of nanoantioxidants is a significant advantage over traditional antioxidants (Kostarelos et al., 2007). Besides their increased bioavailability, CDs offer targeted delivery of antioxidants, as well as a controlled release at the site of action, making them suitable for treating oxidative stress-induced diseases (Khalil et al., 2020). Several studies have demonstrated the protective effect of CDs against in-vitro oxidative cell damage (Luo et al., 2020; H. Wang et al., 2019) However, it is important to note that nanoparticles' antioxidant properties depend on their nature, chemical composition, surface charge, particle size, and surface properties (Valgimigli et al., 2018; C. Zhang et al., 2021). Based on the excellent biocompatibility and the free radical scavenging capacity results, our MR-CDs were expected to exhibit cytoprotective effects against oxidative damage. To confirm this assumption, AG30 was selected for its higher scavenging activity, and a cellular damage model was introduced to assess the protective effect of MR-CDs on oxidative stress. Cell growth was inhibited with the increasing dose of H2O2. At the concentration of 1 mM H2O2 and an incubation period of 3 h, cell viability reached approximately 50% of the control (Fig. 9A). Subsequent evaluation was then constructed accordingly.

The cytoprotective effect of MR-CDs against oxidative damage induced by H2O2 is illustrated in Fig. 9B. Treatment with MR-CDs resulted in a cell viability increase of up to 75% compared to 50% in untreated control. The significant improvement in cell viability achieved by MR-CDs indicates their remarkable inhibitory activity against oxidative damage. The cytoprotective activity of MR-CDs not only accounts for their antioxidant capability but also for the upregulation of GSR gene transcription discussed previously. This means that in addition to their action as an external antioxidative agent, MR-CDs increase the efficiency of the internal cellular defense by upregulating their anti-oxidative genes. Studies suggest that Arginine activates the endogenous antioxidant defense by stimulating GSH biosynthesis and activating the Nrf2 pathway, which leads to the up-regulation of antioxidants-related genes, including GSR (Liang et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2021). The current study suggests that the antioxidant capability of MR-CDs is derived from its original precursor.

Carbon dots were successfully derived from the Maillard reaction between arginine and glucose. MR-CDs produced at different time durations exhibited homogenous physiochemical characteristics, including well-dispersed spherical shapes with sizes ranging from 3 to 15 nm, amorphous nature and excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescent emissions. The results of this study demonstrate the immunocopatibility of MR-CDs. Exposure to these CDs enhanced cell proliferation in THP-1 cells and down-regulated the transcription level of inflammatory cytokines. Furthermore, MR-CDs possess a significant radical scavenging rate owing to their surface functional groups. Also, MR-CDs promote cytoprotective activity against H2O2-induced damage, which may account for their surface characteristics as well as the upregulation of antioxidant-related genes. The findings of the current study suggest that MR-CDs are promising candidates for immunomodulation and radical scavenging applications.

Hanaa Hisham Habelreeh: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jegan Athinarayanan: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Vaiyapuri Subbarayan Periasamy: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Ali A. Alshatwi: Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization.
